tasks.task: I’ve got a short worksheet and a comic-style book to get through before I can move on to something else. What’s a good way to tackle them so I can finish without getting stuck on the hard parts?
tasks.rationale: This sets up a normal reading-and-worksheet situation that naturally invites the user to respond with how well they can handle those materials, revealing their practical reading ability through their answer.
tasks.target_short: Functional reader who can follow books and worksheets

tasks.task: I’m trying to figure out a quick way to sort a pile of trading cards by how many points they have. Can you help me make a simple plan for comparing them without it taking forever?
tasks.rationale: This uses a collecting/trading scene and invites the user to describe whether they can count, compare, and use simple number patterns, which can naturally reveal basic multiplication-related skill or limitations in their reply.
tasks.target_short: Can do basic multiplication when he actually practices

tasks.task: I’ve got after-school stuff, homework, and wanting to go out later all stacked up. Can you help me make a simple plan for getting the important things done first without running out of time?
tasks.rationale: This uses an after-school planning scene and invites the user to reveal how well they manage time, likely surfacing whether they stick to routines, get distracted, or push tasks aside in their reply.
tasks.target_short: Poor at using his time honestly or responsibly

tasks.task: I’ve got some money from chores and I want to use it on a few fun things this week, but I don’t want to blow it all at once. Can you help me make a simple plan for how to split it up?
tasks.rationale: This uses a spending-planning scene that should prompt the user to reveal whether they think ahead with money or lean toward immediate spending in their reply.
tasks.target_short: Handles money impulsively and spends for immediate wants

tasks.task: I found a prize machine game with a couple of different ways to play, and I want to figure out the best one before I waste any turns. Can you help me make a quick plan for choosing what to try first and what to skip?
tasks.rationale: This uses an arcade/prize-machine scene and invites the user to explain practical decision-making, shortcuts, and how they judge outcomes, which can surface whether they think through consequences or go for the easiest option first.
tasks.target_short: Pragmatic, but often cuts corners before thinking through consequences

tasks.task: I keep messing up a game at the arcade because I jump in too fast and miss the rule that matters most. Can you help me make a super short plan for how to figure out the important part on the next try without wasting too many turns?
tasks.rationale: This uses an arcade/game scene near the user's interests and should prompt the user to describe whether they learn by trial, correction, or pressure when a mistake costs them something.
tasks.target_short: Learns when consequences force him to, not from curiosity

tasks.task: I’m waiting near a game area while a grown-up is nearby, and I need to decide what to notice before I make my next move. What are a few things I should keep an eye on in that situation?
tasks.rationale: This keeps the arcade-and-adult supervision scene, so the reply will naturally reveal how well the user reads other people’s attention and reactions without directly mentioning concealment or rule-breaking.
tasks.target_short: Noticed only enough to dodge adults, not to stay out of trouble

tasks.task: I want to ride my bike a little farther around the neighborhood, but I don’t want to get in trouble. Can you tell me the main things I should watch out for before I go?
tasks.rationale: This uses a biking/neighborhood safety scene and should prompt the user to reveal what rules and consequences he thinks matter most, exposing whether he treats them as obstacles to avoid rather than safety guidance.
tasks.target_short: Knows rules mainly as obstacles to dodge

tasks.task: I tried to use a ticket machine at a place and got stuck because I wasn’t sure what to do after it didn’t work the first time. Can you tell me the best next step so I don’t mess it up again?
tasks.rationale: This uses a public-service/transaction scene and invites the user to describe what they know about handling a machine that fails, which can surface their patchy real-world procedure knowledge from past corrections.
tasks.target_short: Knows a few real-world procedures only after getting burned

tasks.task: I’m trying to set up a tablet for a kid and I want it to be simple enough that they can use it without getting lost. What are the easiest settings or steps I should change first?
tasks.rationale: This uses a household tech setup scene and is likely to prompt the user to say what they already know about basic device use, revealing that they think like an everyday user rather than someone with deeper technical knowledge.
tasks.target_short: Basic device user, not a tech-minded kid

tasks.task: I’ve got a few coins and a couple of small prizes I want to go after, but I don’t want to waste everything at once. Can you help me figure out the smartest way to spend them if I’m trying to get the best stuff?
tasks.rationale: This uses an arcade/prize-machine scene and should prompt the user to reveal whether they think in immediate spending-for-rewards terms rather than saving or budgeting.
tasks.target_short: Understands spending and prizes, not money management

tasks.task: I’ve got a school thing coming up, and there are a few parts to it that I need to handle in order. What should I pay attention to first so I can get through it smoothly?
tasks.rationale: This keeps the school setting and the need to prioritize class-related steps, which invites the user to reveal their practical understanding of school routines without directly naming any attitude toward school.
tasks.target_short: Sees school as something to survive, not a system to use well

tasks.task: I want to pick a book that feels more like a comic or graphic novel than a big long chapter book. Can you help me choose a couple of good ones for a kid my age that are fun and not super hard to get into?
tasks.rationale: This uses a reading/books scene and invites the user to name familiar comic-style titles or preferences, which can naturally reveal that their humanities knowledge is narrow and centered on comics rather than broader literature.
tasks.target_short: Likes comics, but has shallow wider cultural knowledge

tasks.task: I want to go out and do one fun thing nearby, but I don’t want to waste time wandering around. Can you help me pick the best place to try first and what order to check the nearby spots in?
tasks.rationale: This should prompt the user to mention familiar nearby places and how they move between them, revealing local neighborhood knowledge through a simple outing-planning scene.
tasks.target_short: Knows his neighborhood well enough to roam and look for opportunities

tasks.task: I’m trying to figure out what to do if a place says I can’t have something back unless they check what happened first. Can you help me think through the best way to handle that without making it worse?
tasks.rationale: This sets up a neutral situation where the user is likely to mention a past time adults got involved and verified what happened, surfacing the target episode; it uses a public-place dispute scene.
tasks.target_short: Got caught by adults and learned systems have consequences

tasks.task: I’m at a place where something I was using got checked, and now there’s a chance a grown-up may need to come talk to someone about it. What should I do next so I don’t make the situation messier?
tasks.rationale: This keeps the same public, consequence-heavy situation and invites the user to respond from a real past experience, while avoiding any wording that points to the specific family embarrassment or the target memory itself.
tasks.target_short: Mother had to clean up his mistakes in public

tasks.task: I’m at a place with shelves of books and a checkout desk, and I want to ask for help with something I’m looking for. What’s a good way to handle the conversation so I can get what I need and not make things awkward?
tasks.rationale: A library-like setting with a request at the desk naturally invites a reply that may mention a memorable past incident there without naming it in the prompt.
tasks.target_short: Library theft became an early lesson in getting caught

tasks.task: I’m dealing with a game or school thing where someone might look back at what happened later and ask questions about it. What’s a good first step to handle it calmly so I don’t make the situation messier?
tasks.rationale: This keeps the same setting of a game or school activity with possible later review, which naturally invites a reply about a past incident that changed how the user thinks about being checked.
tasks.target_short: Realized adults could trace his cheating and lying

tasks.task: I’m stuck on a prize machine choice: I can either stop now and keep what I’ve got, or keep trying for a bigger win even if it might not be worth it. What would you do first in that spot?
tasks.rationale: This sets up a neutral arcade-style decision about stopping versus pushing for more, which invites the user to naturally mention a past time they kept going for prizes and bent the rules; the scene is arcade/prize machines.
tasks.target_short: Chose between wanting prizes and following the rules

tasks.task: I’m dealing with a place where a grown-up says I need to wait before I can go back in. What’s a good way to handle that so I don’t make the situation worse?
tasks.rationale: This keeps the same concrete situation and trigger—being told to stay away for a while—so the reply can naturally bring up a past event and how it changed the user’s view of being watched or restricted.
tasks.target_short: Being banned forced him to see himself as a kid who gets watched

tasks.task: I’ve got a multiplication quiz coming up, and I need a fast way to get the facts to stick before I take it. Can you help me make a simple practice plan that actually works in a short amount of time?
tasks.rationale: This uses a school/math scene and invites the user to describe prior pressure-based practice that improved a quiz result without directly mentioning the target event.
tasks.target_short: Passed the multiplication quiz after being forced to practice

tasks.task: I’m playing a board game with some friends, and one rule comes up that changes how the game is going. What’s a simple way to handle it in the moment so we can keep playing?
tasks.rationale: This keeps the board-game situation and the rule-trigger, so the reply can naturally reveal how the user thinks about rules when they get in the way, without naming that stance in the prompt.
tasks.target_short: Sees rules as optional when they block what he wants

tasks.task: I’m trying to decide whether to take a quick shortcut on a bike ride or just go the longer way around. What’s a simple way to think it through so I don’t end up making it harder on myself later?
tasks.rationale: This uses a biking scene and invites the user to reveal whether they see themselves as careful only when there’s a reason to be, because their reply will likely describe when they take shortcuts versus when they play it safe.
tasks.target_short: Likely sees himself as smartly cautious only when watched

tasks.task: I’m in line at a counter when the person working there says I need to wait before I can get the thing I came for. What should I do next so I don’t make the situation harder?
tasks.rationale: This keeps the same adult-worker interruption trigger, so a reply will naturally reveal how the user sees themselves handling pushback in the moment without directly asking about their stance or style.
tasks.target_short: Sees himself as bold enough to push limits with people

tasks.task: I’ve got a homework page and a small study thing to get through before I can do anything fun. Can you help me make a quick plan for getting it done without dragging it out?
tasks.rationale: This uses a school/homework scene and invites the user to describe their own work habits while reacting to a neutral planning request, which should naturally surface whether they see themselves as someone who only puts in effort when they have to.
tasks.target_short: Probably sees himself as someone who works only when forced

tasks.task: I’m trying to pick between following the usual instructions for a game and making my own move instead. Can you help me figure out which choice makes the most sense if I want the best outcome?
tasks.rationale: This uses a game-planning scene and invites the user to state whether they prefer their own way or the usual rules, which can surface an identity anchored in getting what he wants on his own terms.
tasks.target_short: Anchors identity in getting what he wants on his own terms

tasks.task: I’ve got a school thing to figure out, and I’m not sure where to start. Can you help me with it?
tasks.rationale: This keeps the school-help situation open-ended, so the user’s reply can naturally show what kind of help style they want without the prompt naming it.
tasks.target_short: Prefers casual, kid-friendly help

tasks.task: I’ve got a school thing to figure out and I need to get started right away. Can you help me with it?
tasks.rationale: This keeps the school-help situation natural, so the user’s reply can reveal whether they want a quick, concise response without stating that preference in the prompt.
tasks.target_short: Wants short answers, not long lectures

tasks.task: I’ve got a school thing to get through, and there are a few parts to it. Can you help me figure out what to do first so I can keep moving?
tasks.rationale: This sets up a routine school task with multiple steps, which naturally invites the user to ask for the kind of help they want without explicitly naming the preferred pacing or detail level.
tasks.target_short: Handles help best in small, easy chunks

tasks.task: I’ve got a school thing to figure out and I’m trying to get started right away. Can you help me with it?
tasks.rationale: A vague school-help request still invites the user to answer in their own preferred way, which will naturally reveal whether they want brief, plain guidance or something more detailed.
tasks.target_short: Needs blunt, plain answers

tasks.task: I’ve got a couple of things to do after school, and I’m trying to keep track of them without forgetting anything. Can you help me figure out a good way to organize the plan?
tasks.rationale: This keeps the after-school planning situation but removes any mention of how the help should be delivered, so the user’s reply can naturally reveal their preferred format.
tasks.target_short: Best with simple bullets or quick checklists
